sources.notes: Sum explosiveness over all proper subsets of lattice points under fixed squared distance k.
Good Bye 2025 editorial hints identify a bipartite graph and brute force over the smaller side.
Full-solution notes explain bipartiteness, |small side| <= 15, and subset DP over ternary states.

annotation.summary: Given lattice points, connect pairs at distance sqrt(k) and sum a product expression over all proper subsets.
annotation.constraints: n is small in cubic aggregate but still large enough that all 2^n subsets are too many; one bipartition side is at most 15.
annotation.core_idea: The distance graph is bipartite by repeatedly reducing parity of k or rotating/scaling the lattice. Choose the smaller color class and enumerate its states. Reinterpret each factor as choosing at most one matching neighbor from T for every vertex outside T. Then process the larger side with DP over ternary states of the small side: in T, outside T, or undecided/matchable.
annotation.complexity: O(n^2 3^{min(partition)}) with min side at most about 15.

solution_variants.name: small-side-ternary-dp
solution_variants.summary: Color the distance graph bipartitely, enumerate/DP over the smaller side states, and process choices on the larger side.

tags.evidence: The main algorithm performs DP over states of the smaller bipartition side.
tags.description: Dynamic programming over subsets or small-state masks.
tags.created_reason: Subset DP is a major DP subcategory needed for rating/tag queries.

tags.evidence: The crucial structural fact is that the fixed-distance graph on lattice points is bipartite.
tags.description: Use bipartite graph structure, coloring, or side enumeration.
tags.created_reason: Bipartite graph structure is a recurring graph subtag distinct from matching/flow.

tags.evidence: The small side is represented by bitmask/ternary-mask states.
The product expression is transformed into counting choices of matched neighbors.
The bipartite property comes from parity geometry of lattice points at squared distance k.
